condition.qualitative_description: Total surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct SWBs exist in the MWS, indicating both shrinking storage and an infrastructure gap.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A declining trend in total SWB area combined with a low count of surface water bodies directly indicates that surface irrigation infrastructure is insufficient or deteriorating. In semi-arid Deccan Plateau settings (AER-6), tanks and check dams are the primary surface storage mechanism; their loss or absence leaves farmers dependent on erratic rainfall or costly groundwater lift. Siltation is a key driver of SWB area decline — Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated that a 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat had filled completely with silt at 40 cm/year, eliminating storage capacity entirely.

condition.qualitative_description: The mean ratio of rabi-season SWB area to kharif-season SWB area is below 0.3, indicating that surface water storage dries out rapidly after the monsoon and cannot support rabi or zaid irrigation.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Rapid post-monsoon drying of surface water bodies (low rabi/kharif SWB ratio) is a diagnostic marker of inadequate storage capacity, siltation, or poor tank design. When tanks and check dams lose water quickly after the monsoon, rabi and zaid crops lose their primary surface irrigation source. Dashora et al. (2019) showed that siltation reduces both storage capacity and infiltration rates in check dams, directly undermining their ability to sustain dry-season water availability for agriculture.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA in the MWS, indicating historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA investment in SWC and irrigation works signals that the MWS has not benefited from the public water harvesting programs that are the primary mechanism for expanding surface storage in semi-arid India. Dashora et al. (2019) found a benefit-to-cost ratio of 4.1 for check dams in hard-rock Rajasthan, demonstrating that such investments are economically justified; their absence therefore represents a missed opportunity that amplifies irrigation water scarcity.

condition.qualitative_description: No named river passes through or adjacent to the MWS, eliminating the possibility of diversion or lift irrigation from a perennial or seasonal stream.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The absence of a named river in the MWS means farmers cannot access diversion canals or lift irrigation from a flowing water source. In the Deccan Plateau semi-arid zone, most rivers are ephemeral; where a named river is present it may still support seasonal lift irrigation or check dam construction on a second-order stream. The absence of any river removes this option entirely, making the MWS wholly dependent on tank storage and groundwater, both of which are under stress.

condition.qualitative_description: Nearest agricultural cooperative society is more than 15 km away, indicating poor access to institutional credit for irrigation investment such as pump purchase, pipeline, or micro-irrigation systems.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Distance to cooperative credit institutions constrains farmers' ability to invest in irrigation infrastructure — drip systems, solar pumps, or pipeline networks — even when water is physically available. In Vidarbha and Marathwada, marginal farmers already face high lifting costs from deep wells; without accessible credit, they cannot adopt efficiency-improving technologies. This signal amplifies irrigation challenges but does not confirm water scarcity on its own.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report well or borewell deepening over 5–10 years, or seasonal failure of shallow wells, indicating co-occurring groundwater depletion alongside surface irrigation failure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Well deepening co-confirms groundwater_stress alongside irrigation_challenges when surface storage is inadequate; informs lift-cost assumptions for solar pump solutions.

overall_reasoning_note: Irrigation infrastructure failure in semi-arid Deccan Plateau MWSs should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: declining SWB area trend with low SWB count (sig_01), rapid post-monsoon drying of surface water bodies (sig_02), or very low MGNREGA SWC investment (sig_03). The absence of a river (sig_04) and poor cooperative access (sig_05) are amplifying signals. Siltation of tanks and check dams is a key mechanism linking sig_01 and sig_02 — manual desilting has been shown to increase check dam recharge by 84% (Dashora et al. 2019), making tank desilting through MGNREGA the highest-priority intervention when both signals are present. If SWB area is stable but rabi/kharif ratio is low, prioritise storage design and siltation management over new construction.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing surface water availability independently of infrastructure condition
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm against the long-term semi-arid baseline (~600-800 mm for AER-6). If precipitation has declined sharply in recent years (trend_annual_precipitation_mm strongly negative) and SWB area decline co-occurs with drought years, drought is the primary driver rather than infrastructure failure. Check whether nrega_swc_count is also low — if investment is adequate but SWBs are still drying, drought is more likely the cause.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater depletion (not surface irrigation failure) as the primary cause of water scarcity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If swb_count and swb_total_area_ha are adequate but farmers still report water scarcity, the pathway is more likely groundwater_stress than irrigation_challenges. Confirm by checking whether the MWS has high cropping intensity (mean_cropping_intensity > 1.3) suggesting irrigated double-cropping dependent on groundwater, and ask the user about borewell depth trends (annual_well_depth_m).

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers in your village had to deepen their wells or borewells in the last 5-10 years to find water? If yes, roughly how many metres deeper are they now compared to when the well was first dug or drilled?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If well depth has increased significantly (more than 3-5 metres over the record), this suggests groundwater depletion is co-occurring with surface irrigation failure, strengthening the case for both irrigation_challenges and groundwater_stress pathways. It also indicates that solar pump or lift irrigation solutions must account for higher pumping heads and costs.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Documents check dam hydrology, siltation effects on storage and recharge capacity, benefit-cost ratios (mean 4.1) for hard-rock aquifer settings in monsoonal India, and the 84% recharge increase from manual desilting — directly supporting tank desilting as a priority intervention and siltation as a key driver of SWB area decline.

sources.title: Why is the Arkavathy River drying? A multiple-hypothesis approach in a data-scarce region
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that groundwater pumping and channel fragmentation (including tank and check dam proliferation without maintenance) can reduce surface water availability in Deccan Plateau hard-rock catchments, illustrating how irrigation infrastructure decisions interact with surface water hydrology.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides global context for increasing water scarcity under climate change, relevant to distinguishing meteorological drought from infrastructure-driven irrigation failure in semi-arid India.
